input.background: A computer retailer sells both desktop and laptop personal computers (PCs) online. Assume that 80% of the PCs that the retailer sells online are desktops and 20% are laptops.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{c|cccc}
\hline Table 4.2 & \multicolumn{4}{|c}{ Sample Points for PC Experiment of Example 4.9 } \\
\hline DDDD & $L D D D$ & $L L D D$ & $D L L L$ & $L L L L$ \\
& $D L D D$ & $L D L D$ & $L D L L$ & \\
& $D D L D$ & $L D D L$ & $L L D L$ & \\
& $D D D L$ & $D L L D$ & $L L L D$ & \\
& & $D L D L$ & & \\
& & $D D L L$ & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Find the probability that all of the next four online PC purchases are laptops.

output.answer:
The probability that all four online customers purchase laptops is calculated as follows:
P(All four purchase laptops) = P(LLLL) = (0.2)^4 = 0.0016.
Thus, the probability is only 16 in 10,000 that all four customers purchase laptop PCs.

meta_info.caption: Deriving the Binomial Probability Distribution in a PC Purchase Application
